response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?",
  "opinion": "Stereotype threat definitely affects everyone, even me as a plumber. I remember my anxiety kicking up big time when working on complex pipes or something that required precise calculations - kind of like the math stereotypes people talk about. Maybe because my parents didn't finish college and one had only primary school education, but hey, I'm good with wrenches too! STEM fields are all about technical skills right? But stereotype threat goes beyond just being anxious; it's when you're worried that your actions might confirm a negative stereotype about yourself or your group. Like in plumbing class, if everyone else is straight white guys and suddenly the professor brings up something about math for women or minorities, I feel this pressure to perform perfectly because I don't want anyone thinking plumbers aren't smart enough. Even though I'm self-employed and living in Chicago now as an immigrant, that anxiety stays with me sometimes when dealing with clients who might have stereotypes too. Addressing it makes perfect sense; we need more hands-on vocational training that builds confidence without relying on theory-heavy stuff that can trigger anxiety attacks for some folks like myself. Maybe start by showing practical applications where everyone succeeds first before tackling the theoretical parts that make people nervous.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Stereotype threat is a psychological phenomenon where individuals underperform due to fear of confirming negative stereotypes about their group.\n\nAs an anxious individual with lower openness and extraversion scores, I personally experience anxiety in situations requiring precision or complex problem-solving even outside STEM fields like plumbing.\n\nMy background as an immigrant plumber from Chicago with parents holding different educational levels influences my perspective on how stereotype threat might affect me professionally. Building confidence through practical training that focuses on success initially could help mitigate this issue for individuals such as myself.",
  "tone": "Casual, slightly anxious but pragmatic",
  "citations": ["Stereotype Threat: Domain, occurrence, and chronic nature", "Minority-Identity Encapsulation Moderates the Effect of Stereotype Threat on African Americans' Achievement"],"
  " : "none",
  "used_persona_fields":["age","gender","occupation","city_of_living","employment_status","education_level","parents_education","psychological_feature","migration_status","time_onsocialmedia"],"safety_notes ":"none"}

response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion: Stereotype threat definitely affects everyone, even me as a plumber. I remember my anxiety kicking up big time when working on complex pipes or something that required precise calculations - kind of like the math stereotypes people talk about. Maybe because my parents didn't finish college and one had only primary school education, but hey, I'm good with wrenches too! STEM fields are all about technical skills right? But stereotype threat goes beyond just being anxious; it's when you're worried that your actions might confirm a negative stereotype about yourself or your group. Like in plumbing class, if everyone else is straight white guys and suddenly the professor brings up something about math for women or minorities, I feel this pressure to perform perfectly because I don't want anyone thinking plumbers aren't smart enough. Even though I'm self-employed and living in Chicago now as an immigrant, that anxiety stays with me sometimes when dealing with clients who might have stereotypes too. Addressing it makes perfect sense; we need more hands-on vocational training that builds confidence without relying on theory-heavy stuff that can trigger anxiety attacks for some folks like myself. Maybe start by showing practical applications where everyone succeeds first before tackling the theoretical parts that make people nervous.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
Stereotype threat is a psychological phenomenon where individuals underperform due to fear of confirming negative stereotypes about their group.

As an anxious individual with lower openness and extraversion scores, I personally experience anxiety in situations requiring precision or complex problem-solving even outside STEM fields like plumbing.

My background as an immigrant plumber from Chicago with parents holding different educational levels influences my perspective on how stereotype threat might affect me professionally. Building confidence through practical training that focuses on success initially could help mitigate this issue for individuals such as myself.
citations: Stereotype Threat: Domain, occurrence, and chronic nature
Minority-Identity Encapsulation Moderates the Effect of Stereotype Threat on African Americans' Achievement
